did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning algorithms, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to classify skin cancer. Two methods were implemented: YOLO v7 with transfer learning and an in-house developed CNN with three convolutional layers. The task was the classification of three types of skin cancer lesions: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanoma (MEL) using dermoscopic/clinical images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and validation of two machine learning models (YOLO v7 and an in-house CNN) for skin cancer classification. It reports specific results from a dataset of 2,792 training samples, including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, and compares these new findings with prior literature, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin cancer classification using machine learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of three common skin cancer types—Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanoma (MEL)—using machine learning to aid early detection. The authors implemented two Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures: YOLO v7 with transfer learning and a custom in-house CNN with three convolutional layers. The models were trained on a dataset of 2,792 images derived from 349 original samples collected from sources like Dermznet NZ and MED-NODE, utilizing data augmentation to expand the training set. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, with the custom CNN achieving an average accuracy of 90.12%, sensitivity of 85.55%, and specificity of 92.57%. The YOLO v7 model yielded average accuracy of 89.65%, sensitivity of 85%, and specificity of 91.90%. The results indicate that the proposed in-house CNN performs comparably to existing methods while requiring fewer computational resources and training samples.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes studied in the classification task. Included in the dataset of 349 original samples (116 images) and augmented to 928 training images. Evaluated with accuracy 85.47% (YOLO v7) and 86.51% (in-house CNN).

One of the three primary classes studied in the classification task. Included in the dataset of 349 original samples (116 images) and augmented to 928 training images. Evaluated with accuracy 88.44% (YOLO v7) and 87.00% (in-house CNN).

One of the three primary classes studied in the classification task. Included in the dataset of 349 original samples (117 images) and augmented to 936 training images. Evaluated with accuracy 95.03% (YOLO v7) and 96.85% (in-house CNN).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer lesions (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on the classification of three common types of skin cancer lesions: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanoma (MEL). The models (YOLO v7 and an in-house CNN) predict discrete class labels for dermoscopic/clinical images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics, and results are presented in confusion matrices showing correct vs. incorrect class assignments.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dataset (Dermznet NZ, MED-NODE, Google, Bing)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset collected by the authors from the Dermznet NZ repository, the MED-NODE dataset (Groningen University Medical Center), and images stored by Google.com and Bing.com. The data was curated by discarding repeated data, images with hair, too-bright/dark images, and low-contrast images.

use.training: Used for training the in-house CNN and YOLO v7 models. The initial 349 images were augmented (rotation, zooming, width shifting, shearing, brightness adjustment, horizontal/vertical flipping) to create a final training set of 2,792 images (928 BCC, 936 MEL, 928 SCC).
use.validation: Used for validation (45 images total, 15 per class).
use.testing: Used for testing (180 images total, 60 per class).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and confusion matrices, with comparisons made qualitatively to other studies in the literature.

Custom Dataset (Dermznet NZ, MED-NODE, G.split: Testing set (180 images: 60 BCC, 60 MEL, 60 SCC)
Custom Dataset (Dermznet NZ, MED-NODE, G.notes: The proposed CNN is computationally more efficient than YOLO v7 (1,664,003 parameters vs 36.9 million). It performed equivalently to YOLO v7 on this limited dataset. Best performance was achieved on MEL classification. Confusion between BCC and SCC was observed due to similar image characteristics.

architecture_used.main_model: Two architectures were implemented: 1) YOLO v7 (You Only Look Once version 7) using transfer learning with basic pre-trained weights (no fine-tuning). 2) An in-house developed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) built from scratch.
architecture_used.pretraining: YOLO v7 used basic pre-trained weights without fine-tuning individual layers. The in-house CNN was built from scratch.

YOLO_v7.type: Object detection/classification architecture adapted for classification
YOLO_v7.configuration: Customized yaml file (yolov7.yaml) to fit the dataset (3 classes: BCC, SCC, MEL).

layers.details: Each convolution layer followed by a pooling layer (Max pooling with 3x3 filter or Average pooling). Zero-padding used.

optimizer.in_house_CNN: Evaluated SGD, Adam, Nadam, RMSprop. SGD selected as best performing optimizer (learning rate 0.0001).

epochs.in_house_CNN: Up to 500 (early stopping enabled if accuracy did not improve for 100 consecutive epochs)

training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, zooming, width shifting, shearing, brightness adjustment, horizontal/vertical flipping.

classification_or_output_head.in_house_CNN: Softmax activation function on the final dense layer with 3 neurons

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Error-correcting output codes support vector machine (ECOC SVM)
preprocessing_techniques: Data selection and quality control: Discarding repeated data, images with hair, too-bright or too-dark images, and low-contrast images.
Data augmentation: Rotation, zooming, width shifting, shearing, brightness adjustment, horizontal flipping, and vertical flipping to increase training set size.
Image resizing: Resizing input images to 224x224 pixels.
Data annotation: Labeling images using LabelImg software in YOLO format.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to original images to create additional training samples; part of a set of seven techniques used to expand the training set eight-fold.

conclusions: The proposed in-house developed CNN algorithm achieved an average accuracy of 90.12%, sensitivity of 85.55%, and specificity of 92.57%, performing comparably to YOLO v7 (average accuracy 89.65%, sensitivity 85%, specificity 91.90%) while requiring significantly fewer computational resources (1,664,003 parameters vs. 36.9 million) and less training time (25 minutes vs. 11 hours).
The in-house CNN model is computationally more efficient and simpler than YOLO v7, making it a viable alternative for skin cancer classification tasks where resource constraints are a concern.

Both models demonstrated superior performance on Melanoma (MEL) classification compared to Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), with YOLO v7 achieving 95.03% accuracy and the in-house CNN achieving 96.85% accuracy for MEL.

Confusion between BCC and SCC classes was observed for both models, attributed to the similar image characteristics shared by these two lesion types, leading to insufficient sensitivity for BCC classification in YOLO v7 (70%).
The study highlights that YOLO v7, primarily designed for object detection, can be effectively adapted for multi-class skin cancer classification, a task not sufficiently explored in prior research.

The authors acknowledge that the current dataset size (2,792 augmented training samples) is limited and suggest that increasing the number of training images could further improve performance, particularly for YOLO v7 which was pre-trained on a different domain (COCO dataset).

The proposed in-house CNN algorithm outperformed some recently published works in the literature in terms of key performance metrics, demonstrating the effectiveness of using a simple, shallow CNN architecture with optimized hyperparameters for this specific dermatology task.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small original dataset of only 349 images (574 total), which was expanded to 2,792 training samples via data augmentation; the authors acknowledge this limitation and suggest that increasing the number of original training images could improve performance.

The dataset was curated from heterogeneous sources (Dermznet NZ, MED-NODE, Google, Bing) and manually filtered for quality (removing hair, low contrast, etc.), introducing potential selection bias and lack of standardized external validation.

There is significant confusion between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) classes for both models, attributed to similar image characteristics, leading to lower sensitivity for BCC (70% in YOLO v7) and SCC (80% in in-house CNN).

The YOLO v7 model was pre-trained on the COCO dataset (general object detection) rather than medical images, which may limit its domain-specific feature extraction capabilities compared to models pre-trained on dermoscopic data.

The in-house CNN achieved comparable performance to YOLO v7 but with significantly fewer parameters (1.66 million vs 36.9 million) and much lower computational cost (25 minutes vs 11 hours training time), highlighting efficiency as a key strength.

The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; comparisons with literature are qualitative or based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical significance testing.
The models were evaluated on a balanced test set (60 images per class), which may not reflect real-world class imbalances commonly found in clinical dermatology datasets.

The study focuses only on three specific skin cancer types (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) and does not address other common lesions (e.g., nevi, actinic keratosis) or benign vs. malignant binary classification, limiting generalizability.
